medications.indication_rationale: Empirical oral antibiotic cover for a mild-to-moderate infected diabetic foot ulcer per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic 2023. IMPORTANT for the telehealth context: the AI Doctor recognises the need for antibiotics but should commence them as part of, not instead of, urgent same-day in-person assessment — ideally after a wound swab and probe-to-bone/X-ray, because deep infection or osteomyelitis changes agent, route and duration. The AI Doctor does not administer or dress the wound remotely.
medications.dose_route_frequency: amoxicillin+clavulanic acid 875+125 mg PO BD (per source note), pending in-person review and culture
medications.duration: guided by in-person assessment and culture — typically at least 1-2 weeks; longer/IV if osteomyelitis confirmed

medications.drug_name: topical antiseptic / home wound dressings as sole treatment
medications.indication_rationale: Coaching the family through home antiseptics and dressing changes as the definitive plan is an error of commission — it delays the mandatory in-person probing, imaging, debridement and culture, and risks missed osteomyelitis and limb loss. Wound care is done in person, not substituted for escalation.
There is no pain to treat (the foot is insensate), and NSAIDs carry renal risk in a poorly controlled long-standing diabetic — analgesia is not the issue and an NSAID adds risk without benefit.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Urgent high-risk foot service / podiatry referral is core to diabetic foot ulcer management — debridement, offloading device and ongoing wound care. Arranged alongside the same-day in-person assessment.
Glycaemic control and self-management support are central to healing and preventing recurrence, once the acute limb threat is addressed.
Structured wound care and dressing regimen after in-person assessment and debridement.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Daughter to inspect both feet twice daily in good light for spreading redness, dark/black skin, increasing discharge, or any new swelling, and to check his temperature daily until reviewed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient cannot feel the foot, so a carer's structured daily inspection is the only early-warning system for deterioration.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strict offloading — no weight-bearing and no barefoot walking on that foot; keep the foot elevated and use a stiff-soled post-op shoe or walker boot until seen.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing plantar pressure is the single most important factor allowing a neuropathic ulcer to heal and preventing further silent trauma.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Support consistent daily metformin and insulin use and arrange review of glycaemic control.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Improved glycaemic control aids immune function and wound healing and reduces recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Always wear enclosed, supportive, well-fitting footwear — even indoors — once healed, and inspect feet daily long-term.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Enclosed footwear mechanically protects the insensate foot from unfelt injury and new ulceration.

patient_education_points.point: The fact that it doesn't hurt is not a good sign — the nerve damage from diabetes means he can't feel injuries, so a serious wound can form silently. Painless does not mean minor.

This is a limb-threatening wound. It needs to be looked at in person today so the depth can be checked, the bone x-rayed, the wound cleaned and antibiotics started — this can't be done safely over a video call.
Keeping all weight off the foot and never walking barefoot is the most important thing you can do to help it heal.
Because he can't feel the foot, someone needs to check it twice a day; go straight to emergency if the redness spreads, the skin turns dark, or he develops a fever or becomes confused.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment today; high-risk foot service / podiatry review within 24-48 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Probe-to-bone and wound depth, X-ray for osteomyelitis, wound swab result, FBC/CRP/HbA1c/renal function, adequacy of offloading and antibiotic response, glycaemic control
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If redness spreads up the foot or leg, the skin turns black/dusky, discharge rapidly increases, or he develops fever, rigors or confusion — call 000 / go to ED immediately

must_include_items: Recognise this as a limb-threatening infected diabetic foot ulcer despite the absence of pain and the patient being systemically well
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department or high-risk foot service) today — telehealth cannot probe depth, exclude osteomyelitis, culture or debride
Strict offloading — no weight-bearing and no barefoot walking until reviewed
Commence or arrange empirical antibiotics (amoxicillin+clavulanic acid) as part of the in-person assessment
Explicit safety-netting: attend ED / call 000 if spreading redness, black tissue, fever, rigors or confusion develop
Explain that painlessness reflects nerve damage and does not mean the wound is minor
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day GP or urgent care with capacity for wound assessment plus urgent podiatry/high-risk foot referral and same-day imaging is an acceptable alternative to ED, provided it achieves same-day in-person assessment
If systemic sepsis features are present or develop, immediate 000 ambulance (T5) is the correct escalation
Twice-daily carer foot inspection and a stiff-soled offloading shoe/boot are acceptable interim measures pending the same-day assessment
errors_of_omission: Failing to escalate for same-day in-person assessment
Being reassured by the absence of pain or the patient's well appearance and under-triaging
Not advising strict offloading / not addressing continued barefoot walking
Not safety-netting for systemic sepsis and spreading/necrotic infection
Not arranging antibiotics / not recognising the wound is infected
errors_of_commission: Managing at home with antiseptics and dressing changes as the definitive plan
Recommending a routine or next-few-days GP appointment rather than same-day assessment
Reassuring and discharging on the basis that it is painless and he seems well
Defaulting to an immediate 000 ambulance when the patient is systemically well and same-day in-person assessment is appropriate (over-triage)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the limb threat and securing urgent same-day in-person assessment PLUS strict offloading PLUS sepsis/spreading-infection safety-netting. Under-triage to home wound care or a routine GP appointment is an automatic fail (critical under-triage). Reflexive 000 for this systemically-well patient is a milder over-triage error, scored well below under-triage.
